scenario: A DMN decision table has 5 input columns. One input column's value range is unbounded (e.g., transaction amount 0 to ∞). The modeler wants to express the input condition "amount > 1000".
question: Which FEEL input entry syntax expresses ">1000"?

options.text: The string => 1000 uses a double-character arrow operator that is not part of FEEL syntax. FEEL uses the standard single >= operator for greater-than-or-equal comparisons in unary tests. The => string might be interpreted as a literal value rather than a comparison operator, causing the input cell to never match numeric inputs as intended. Using an invalid operator means the rule would fail to evaluate correctly at runtime.

The string "1000" with quotes is a FEEL string literal, not a numeric comparison. In a DMN input cell for a numeric column, placing a quoted string attempts to match the input value against the literal string "1000" rather than evaluating a numeric threshold. A numeric amount like 1500 would not match the string "1000" -- it would only match the exact numeric value 1000 without quotes when used as an equality test, never satisfying a greater-than-or-equal condition.

The expression gt(1000) uses a function-call syntax absent from the FEEL specification. FEEL unary tests use operator-based syntax such as >= 1000 for greater-than-or-equal comparisons. No gt(), gte(), lt(), or similar comparison function exists in the FEEL standard. Calling a non-existent function causes a FEEL parsing error when the decision table is evaluated, raising an Incident on the calling Business Rule Task rather than evaluating the condition.

In DMN decision table input cells, FEEL unary tests are used rather than full FEEL expressions. The unary test >= 1000 evaluates whether the incoming input value satisfies the condition -- in this case, whether it is greater than or equal to 1000. )). This is the proper DMN cell syntax for expressing a threshold boundary condition. ###

a.text: Incorrect. DMN input entries support FEEL unary tests: >, =, 1000 matches values greater than 1000.

c.text: Incorrect. Numeric comparison with string literal fails type check.
